source_phrase: Brush your teeth twice a day

interrogatives.who: Human hosts, oral microbiota, caregivers, dental professionals, and institutions that transmit hygiene norms.
interrogatives.what: Bacteria are living microorganisms that can inhabit the mouth and contribute to plaque and decay under certain conditions.
interrogatives.when: They matter when conditions allow growth, accumulation, acid production, or harmful interaction with teeth and gums.
interrogatives.where: They exist in the oral cavity, especially on tooth surfaces, gums, tongue, and biofilm-forming areas.
interrogatives.how: They reproduce, form biofilms, metabolize available nutrients, and can create byproducts that affect enamel, gums, odor, and disease risk.
interrogatives.why: They create governance pressure because individual daily behavior can reduce harmful accumulation before damage becomes costly or painful.
interrogatives.why_not: They may become less relevant if other controls prevent harmful accumulation, if the host lacks teeth, or if the biological conditions for harm are absent. These exceptions require evidence.

prerequisite_truths.claim: Bacteria can exist in the human mouth.
prerequisite_truths.notes: Common biological premise; source citation to be added in evidence pass.

prerequisite_truths.claim: Some oral bacteria can contribute to plaque, acid production, decay, gum disease, or odor.
prerequisite_truths.notes: Requires dental/public-health sources in evidence pass.

prerequisite_truths.claim: Daily tooth brushing can reduce harmful accumulation.
prerequisite_truths.notes: Requires dental/public-health sources in evidence pass.

evidence_notes: Evidence citations are intentionally deferred until the first evidence pass.
ambiguity_zones: Twice daily may be a practical public-health compression rather than a universal biological constant.
Different populations, diets, dental conditions, and tools may alter the optimal behavior.
